sentences.text: Yes.
I wanted to tell you that.
Because that was terribly moving to me.
We were, again, on the upper [NON-ENGLISH], and there were megaphones playing beautiful music.
One of them was [NON-ENGLISH], which is a very emotional song connected with mother's heart-- that only mother's heart can understand children.
And there were still quite a few children, because the ghetto was liquidated, and a lot of children-- not a lot-- but some of the children were smuggled into Plaszow.
And the people arrive in March.
And in May was that selection.
And they have collected all the children-- tore them away from they mothers.
SS walk amongst us and have taken the childrens.
Nobody saw anybody ever since.
None of the children have survived.
They have taken also elderly women.
A lot of policeman brought they parents, they mothers, olderly mothers.
They thought because they are policemens that they will be able to save they family.
And some of them did.
And because of that, they have undertaken that sort of job.
Some of them were power-hungry.
Some of them were getting more food.
But the main thing was to survive.
But that-- that was a terrible-- to live through.
I don't know exactly how many.
But there were a few trucks of children.
There must have been quite a few-- and because they also took elderly people or people looking sickly-- they have taken.
So it's very difficult for me to tell how many.
But there must be somewhere evidence of that.
Because that was incident that did concern a lot of people.
For a long time we were talking about it.
Not at that stage.
They were told distinctly that they're going to orphanage.
So some of the mothers volunteered.
There was a woman, Mrs.
Nachsets, a dentist's wife.
And I know she gave her Teresca quite OK.
Teresca, you will be looked after as they explained.
You know, there was still that naivete amongst us-- believe what we wanted to believe-- that they were telling the truth.
So-- and that was terrible.
That was right at the beginning.
And from then on, it was worse, and worse, and worse.
And the only way I feel I have survived-- because I have hidden myself.
I-- I told you my mother was taken away.
I was convinced then that my mother is not alive anymore, although that was not the case.
[SIGHS] Mother went to Skarzysko-Kamienna.
My father certainly was not alive anymore.
So I had no reason to-- to care, no reason to worry about anything anymore.
One didn't want to suffer.
After Plaszow we were taken to Auschwitz.
1,600 of us, we were taken in cattle trucks, locked, with barbed wire over the little windows.
I don't remember.
I really don't remember.
But it didn't take very long.
I remember seeing the villagers at that stage.
There were whispers that we going to Auschwitz.
But I didn't quite know the route.
And it didn't matter.
It really didn't matter anymore.
You know, one acquired that quiet defiance and resignation.
It didn't matter.
At one moment, you care.
You wanted to live.
When you came so close to dying, you wanted to live.
But there was a word, [NON-ENGLISH].
You're going to die anyhow.
So what-- what does it matter?
The sooner the better.
In the wagons, I stood near the window.
I always make point of being near the windows.
Because the girls knew that I was tall enough to see through to read the names of the villages we were passing by.
And at one stage, when we moved to Podgorze, I had piece of paper-- brown piece of paper.
I have written to my friend, Zoska-- to be deliver to Zoska Woitashek.
And everybody knew it-- Podgorze, Zoska Woitashek-- a brown piece of paper.
I have found out afterwards, when I met with Zoska many, many years later, she found the piece of paper.
And I have written on it that we're going to Auschwitz.
So we knew.
But again, one didn't want to believe.
Because we felt from Auschwitz you only come as in ashes back home.
There were some ashes sent to different people who were not dead even.
And of course, a lot of them were dead.
There was a case of a fellow called Ginsberg.
They sent ashes and wallet to the wife, Bronka Ginsberg, telling her that the husband is dead.
So she took as, you know, fact that he is dead.
I think she really remarried somebody else.
And when she came to Auschwitz herself, she found that he's alive.
So there was such a macabre incidents.
Like animals, we were herded out of the trains.
We straightened our legs with great difficulties.
Because in every carriage there were 500 of us.
[SIGHS] And quick, quick, quick we marched somewhere.
And we could see the faces of the people in the blocks-- fairly decently dressed.
Later, we found out that the blocks were called Canada.
They were well-dressed, because they worked in a store rooms.
The clothes we arrived with, they were segregating-- cleaning, dry cleaning.
Some of it they took for themselves.
And-- and the rest was packed, and it was sent to Germany.
Well, we eventually reached the block.
We were told to get undressed-- on one pile to put clothing; on another pile to put shoes.
And if we had anything else to give-- jewelry, anything-- we had no jewelry by then-- nobody.
I certainly didn't have anything to start with, never mind then.
And again, there were two rows of SS men.
And we had to go through undressed.
My mother always taught me to straighten myself and know that I'm always 18 years old, whatever-- whenever they caught me and asked me how old I am.
And I went amongst the first few rows-- straight.
And one of the SS men grabbed my arm and ask me, [GERMAN]?
Hold old are you?
I was thin.
I had no bust.
I was skinny.
And I said, [GERMAN].
And I straightened myself up.
And as I said, [GERMAN]--
I had a little photo montage-- no bigger than that-- in my mouth, under my tongue.
Well, I wanted to preserve it.
It was family-- a picture of my family-- tiny little heads of the five of us-- my father, my mother, my sister, my brother, and myself-- that I wanted to preserve.
So I put under my tongue.
And as I spoke, 18, this came out of my mouth.
And he started whipping into me, kicking me.
[NON-ENGLISH].
He sent me to the left, where there already a few people collected from the first few rows who were older, and thin, and didn't look as--
I understood that that was the punishment, and that's the oven-- where we knew we were in Auschwitz.
And we didn't seen anybody survive.
But maybe for a little while, they will let us live.
So-- [SIGHS] I have taken hold of myself.
I picked myself up.
There were 1,600 of us in that particular transport.
And I went back into the rows of people who were approaching the SS men.
And I went through from the other side.
And I thought, if I get through, I get through.
If I don't get through, well-- surely, they did not see us as people.
They have seen us as cattle.
He won't remember my features, my face.
And I went through.
And so I found myself waiting in the great, big room, this size, squeezed like sardines, waiting to find out if the gas will come.
There were little facets-- faucets-- or water, or gas could come out.
And we were also given a tiny square of soap-- which we knew it was soap.
It was, again, you know, the rumors.
There were always people who were always ready to enlighten us.
And each piece of soap had a certain number on it, from which transport, or whatever.
We even--
That it was human fat-- that the people who were gassed, who were sent to the ovens-- somehow they were collecting the fat of the humans the same way as they were collecting hair-- human hair.
And they were doing things with it.
So it was the human soap.
We even used to-- to sort of wash ourselves with it knowing that it's human fat.
You know, right through the thing, you-- you sort of pushed away.
You didn't want to know.
You didn't want to believe it.
It-- it couldn't be that-- that terrible.
It couldn't be that bad.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We went through the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --washing period.
I think maybe a few minutes it took-- wet-- cold-- pushing each other.
There were SS women giving clothes.
And you had to pick a piece of clothes-- article of clothes-- in a running pace.
I have managed to picked up a green dress, evening dress, with open front, open back-- no sleeves-- a pair of knickers, and pair of clogs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was glad to have something.
And as we walked on, we went through a place where there were autoclaves.
And I spotted a fellow, also a prisoner, working with the autoclaves, pushing, pulling clothes from one autoclave to another autoclave.
And I spotted a coat-- a big coat-- men's clothing.
And today, to this day, I realize that it was not Polish coat.
It was beautiful coat-- pure wool.
It had fur collar.
I grabbed the coat.
And I said, please give me that coat.
I knew that with my figure, I won't exist very long in Auschwitz in an evening dress-- full length, and here-- nothing!
And he said, I can't give you the coat.
You get shot.
It's got a fur collar.
I said, I'll get shot anyhow.
Give me that coat.
And he fought with me.
And I fought with him.
But he was little.
And my life depended on it.
I grabbed the coat.
And the rest was following me and telling me, go on, go on, quick!
Because you know, that was the attitude.
Don't stop the queue.
They'll shoot you!
They'll shoot us!
I grabbed the coat.
I had the coat in Auschwitz.
I was the only one walking in a men's winter coat-- with a fur coat.
I was wearing it up to make sure they didn't see.
But nobody cared anymore.
We were all destined for ovens sooner or later.
It just probably that particular period we were still to work.
Sure enough, we were in barrack.
It was Birkenau-- [SIGHS] --cold, freezing, empty wooden boards.
And that's how we slept, like sardines, in the clothes we were wearing.
One turn around, we all had to turn around.
In the morning, we woke up.
It was Saturday.
Nobody needed us.
There was no work.
And we were waiting the first few days.
What will happen to us?
What do they want with us?
In the afternoon, they were giving food-- consisted of some bowl or cup of that cold, so-called soup, a piece of bread-- on this hand, a spoonful of jam, and here, margarine.
We were totally surprised-- jam and margarine before they're going to gas us?
What's happened?
Well, I devised a mode of getting more of that bread, and jam, and bread.
And I was with Hellga Seigal, the woman who has adopted me.
I forgot to tell you that my mother survived in Skarzysko-- through a coincident, took care of her daughter, her-- Hellga Seigal's daughter.
And Hellga Seigal adopted me and took care of me, sharing her food with me, because she felt that I need more to eat than she did.
And very often I would bring bread to her.
When I was scrubbing the blocks, I came with extra bread.
So we-- we had a very good rapport, a very good relationship.
That lady died-- she was 85-- in Israel.
I always would go and stay with her and visit her when I went to Israel.
[SIGHS] But on that time, that-- this time-- in Auschwitz, they would close the block, get us all out, one front door-- lock it.
And one would stand.
One block [NON-ENGLISH] would stand there not to let anybody out.
And the others were dividing the food, dealing the food to us.
Well, I would go on the third bunk where we were asleep.
There was the window level with our bunk.
I would jump down.
It was like this window here.
And I slept level with that window-- go down and queue again-- lick off that jam and butter, eat up this bread.
And poor Hellga was always terrified that I'd get caught.
But there was nobody on the sides of the block.
They were standing at the end and by the front door and the back door.
Probably be beaten up to pulp.
But they were all Jewish women dishing out the food.
There were no SS women in our compound.
They wouldn't go in.
It was full of lice.
And-- and we were in a desperate state.
Yes, I did.
I forgot to tell you.
Before we went into the wash, after we were selected, we did get a number.
I have a number.
Like cattle, when the cattle is being branded-- when I see on television sometime-- I always compare the way they tattoo very nicely-- tattooed.
And some of the women who were doing the tattooing didn't have such a nice writing.
So I said to one girl which stood with me, I said, come on.
We'll go to her.
She's doing on the inside.
When you get on the outside, it's horrible to this day.
I think to myself, you know, constantly I use my intellect, instinct, survival instinct, to-- to do the right thing by me as much as I could-- not that that helped me to survive.
Because there were smarter, more intelligent, cleverer people did not survive.
But there were those little things that, you know-- sort of almost like playing with them.
Because just to see, to get out of one row of people to another row-- there were SS women.
They would whip you to pulp.
Well, it didn't matter.
We thought-- [SIGHS] that's it.
Yes.
Yes.
They did very nice job for me--
--very neat and on the inside of the arm.
See?
This is my number.
It's-- sort of it was more
like a-- like-- like cats and like playing-- like a kitten playing with a ball of wool-- that was my attitude to them.
It-- it-- just-- disregard them.
Make fun of them.
But it was no fun.
We--
Hard.
They did-- plenty of times, plenty of time.
At one stage, a few days after we arrived there, a SS women came to choose people to work.
So we did volunteer to work.
See, it was always better to go to work than to sit and-- and become desperate.
To go to work, it meant a little bit of better clothes, and also more to eat.
That's what we thought.
Sometimes, it was like that, but not very often.
And I volunteered to go to [NON-ENGLISH].
It's a sewing compound.
We were taken through fields.
When I saw that film, I remember that much.
It was so well-portrayed.
That's why I always remember everything dark.
We walked.
Our feet were sinking into that mud.
And we went to Auschwitz proper.
We live in blocks.
It was called Munsterlager.
Munsterlager, it meant a camp for show-- the part of the Auschwitz which was shown to Swiss Red Cross.
And that's the part I have found myself in.
First of all, we came to Auschwitz.
We were washed.
We were given some clothes.
I lost that beautiful fur coat-- that coat-- winter coat with the fur.
But still, I got some other clothes, shoes.
And we lived in a block-- believe it or not-- had a red, polished floors.
We work in sewing factory.
We were repairing soldiers' uniforms from Eastern front.
Very often, there was a coat with a hole, which we raised quickly to show to the girls that they should rejoice-- that somebody got killed, that they're getting back the Germans.
There were sometimes a army thing, because that was already clean, dry cleaned, to us brought just to sew.
And very often, we would use lining to-- to make something for us to wear underneath that prison uniform, to keep a little bit warmer.
And after the work-- I was on night shift.
We always went back to the block.
I, again, I slept on the top bunk.
And you were given some paper money in that Munsterlager, in that exhibition block.
There were men in the next camp.
But we were divided with the barbed wire.
But you could talk to them.
You could find out where they are from, who is amongst them.
And sometimes, girls found they brothers.
[SIGHS] [SIGHING] It was very sad, very tragic.
We were not allowed to get down during the day to go to toilet.
And the cold made us want to go to toilet.
So we would use a little canister to urinate in the-- on the bunk-- and empty it in the evening before we went to work.
One had to be always careful that one doesn't eat from the same utensil that you were urinating to.
There were all modes of survival.
You can't sleep if your bladder is full.
You can't-- you just can't, no matter how tired you are.
Sometimes that thing would spill.
And the girls from down below kept saying, hey, what's the matter?
Something is leaking.
And we would say, yes.
It's coffee.
The one on the third bunk-- they always had coffee.
How they preserved the coffee, I'll never know.
What the-- all the ones on top always had coffee.
That's what we said.
We had to say it.
Because how can we tell that that was urine?
And so we were going backwards and forwards to that [NON-ENGLISH].
It didn't take all that long.
But somehow I managed on that Munsterlager to get in touch with the girls which we're going to laundry.
And I thought, always being adventurous, I want to go to the laundry once.
So we swapped.
She went to do sewing.
And I went to laundry.
And the laundry group was very small.
But it was nice and warm-- and water and soap.
And you could wash your clothes while you were there-- put it to autoclaves, dry it.
So it was very good.
I don't know whoever else did that.
But I did.
When I saw an opportunity to pinch some of the clothes that we were supposed to wash and dry, it was better to what I was wearing.
So I put it on-- quite a few garments.
And on the way back home, I looked under that thing, which was belonging to the camp, that striped thing.
I have put quite a few dresses-- don't know how many-- maybe 4, 5, 6.
On myself, of course.
That was the only way to get something through.
And the SS men were counting us, if God forbid, nobody disappeared.
Because I would have said the laundry or somewhere outside our compound.
And he noticed that I look fat-- too fat for my face and too fat for my legs.
And he came to me.
And he told me to undo the thing.
I did.
And he looked.
And he said, [GERMAN].
Undress.
I did. 1, 2, 3-- he was counting and whipping me.
And I said, now I really have done it-- 4, 5, 6-- and they started to laugh.
They were army.
They were not SS.
They were outside the Auschwitz compound.
That was a proper loan.
[SIGHS] I had to get everything off.
I was just left in one item.
But I was beaten.
I put back my jacket on.
And I went back to my block, to my Munsterlager, to my prison.
Never again did I go to laundry.
Not only that I wasn't allowed to go anymore, I didn't want to go anymore.
Back to [NON-ENGLISH], we were sitting there.
It was plenty to sew.
Till one morning, we came from work.
And they told us not to go into the block, but to go to the quadrangle.
It was a place between blocks, between two, three blocks, where we were collecting, always to be counted.
There was a gallow.
And there were four girls hanging.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And there was a fellow standing and using these four bodies hanged, a [NON-ENGLISH] guard, pushing with the whip the bodies, so that they would sway.
To make a bigger impact upon us.
Because the bodies were already hanged during the day in the morning, for the benefit of the day shift.
We were on the night shift.
And we saw it when we came back from the night shift.
Because, I was told then, that they tried to steal a little bit of powder-- gunpowder-- to detonate the oven, to detonate the chimney.
Well, I believe they did.
They did do some damage.
That's how they found out that they were stealing powder.
[SIGHS] I have an article.
One of the-- there is an article I have here.
I can show you.
You will get the names.
It was-- I think this was terribly sad what they did to four young girls.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And afterwards, they died already, to use them to terrified us more-- to impress upon us what will happen if we steal something, and especially gunpowder.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was a terrible experience to see that.
That lives with me.
I can never dismiss that from my eyes.
When I see Western film, I'm turning off before the lynching and hanging start.
When I just see that the cowboy's going near a tree, I turn off the television.
Because I have those four girls constantly with me in front of my eyes.
[SIGHS]
No.
They were really a part of a resistance, weren't they?
They were trying to undermine--
Well, they must have been part of the resistance, because they were taking that power somehow out of the Auschwitz to get to the resistance, to the-- to the people who were using that powder.
But because we didn't know about the resistance then, one didn't know anything.
We didn't know the time of day.
We thought that they were using it for the chimney inside.
Of course they were Jewish girls.
They were four Jewish, Slovak girls.
Show me too-- let me show you-- to show you the pictures and the names of the girls.
I've got it here.
Do you want to see it?
OK.
